nativeRange.habitat: Small rivers and brooks under rainforest cover and forested savannah; schooling fish found in the flowing sections of creeks over soils of basal origin. Upper water column near the surface.

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and background (Anubias, Java fern, fine-leaved stem plants)
dark substrate to intensify iridescent coloration
floating plants to diffuse surface light
driftwood and leaf litter for cover and tannins
cork bark or spawning mops as egg-laying sites

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous; strongly prefers small live and frozen micro-foods. Daphnia, white worms, fruit flies (Drosophila), mosquito larvae, bloodworms, and brine shrimp (Artemia) are all accepted. Will also take high-quality micro-pellets and crushed flake once acclimated, but live or frozen foods are essential for conditioning and long-term health. Feed small amounts 1–2 times daily at or near the surface.

goodWith: other small West African killifish (Epiplatys, Aphyosemion spp.)
small peaceful tetras of similar size
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding
predatory fish that can swallow 5 cm fish
fast-moving species that stress timid surface dwellers

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo summary page for Procatopus aberrans (species ID 9702). Check for individual photo license details on the page.

summary: A diminutive West African lampeye killifish from Nigeria and Cameroon, males display brilliant blue-green iridescence on the flanks and a copper-red iris unique among lampeyes. Non-annual; short-lived at 1–2 years. Intermediate care: soft, acidic water and a tightly covered tank are essential.
careNotes: Procatopus aberrans, the Bluegreen Lampeye, is a small non-annual killifish from rainforest streams and forested savannah brooks in Cameroon and Nigeria. Males display a striking blue-green metallic sheen along the flanks and a copper-red iridescent eye zone that distinguishes them from most other lampeye species, which have a blue iris. A group of at least 6 individuals is recommended; the species is a surface-oriented schooling fish and will be stressed in isolation or small numbers. Because P. aberrans schools near the surface and is an accomplished jumper, a tight-fitting cover glass is non-negotiable — gaps of a few millimetres are sufficient for escape. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water is essential: pH 6.5–7.2, gH 2–10, kH 1–5. The species is sensitive to deteriorating water quality and responds poorly to hard or alkaline conditions; regular small water changes using RO or soft tap water are advisable. A dark substrate and dark background will elicit the strongest coloration. The family Procatopodidae has been elevated from a subfamily of Poeciliidae and is recognised as a distinct family of African lampeye killifishes. P. aberrans is listed as Least Concern by the IUCN but is infrequently seen in the trade. FishBase notes it as “very difficult to maintain in aquarium”; intermediate care is appropriate for dedicated killifish keepers who can supply live or frozen micro-foods and stable soft-water conditions. Lifespan is short at 1–2 years.
breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner that deposits 1–3 eggs daily in hidden crevices, among plant roots, cork bark, spawning mops, or filter foam. Eggs take 14–40 days to hatch depending on temperature (shorter at 26°C, longer toward 22°C). Fry are 4–5 mm at hatching and require very fine first foods: infusoria, micro worms, or vinegar eels for the first week, progressing to baby brine shrimp nauplii and finely crushed flake. Adults will consume fry; a separate rearing vessel or dense cover is advisable. Spawning medium (cork bark or mop) can be transferred to a dedicated fry tank weekly. Breeding difficulty is generally rated easy to moderate once stable water conditions are established and live foods are supplied regularly.

primary.name: FishBase — Procatopus aberrans (species ID 9702)
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed max size 5.5 cm TL, temperature 24–26°C, pH 6.5–7.2, native range (Cameroon — Cross River basin; Nigeria — Niger and Benue Rivers), habitat (small rivers/brooks under forest cover and forested savannah), taxonomy (Procatopodidae, Cyprinodontiformes), trophic level 3.2 (carnivore), non-migratory freshwater species, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Have You Heard about the Procatopus aberrans?
additional.notes: Detailed hobbyist article. Confirmed male size ~75 mm, female ~50 mm; native habitat temperature 20–24°C; soft acidic water for breeding; 35–40 day incubation period; 4–5 mm fry size; strong preference for daphnia and white worms; notes species is short-lived; males chase subordinates; excellent escape artists.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Blue-green Lampeye (Procatopus aberrans)
additional.notes: Practical Fishkeeping trade source. Adult size 5.5 cm; temp 24–26°C; pH 6.5–7.5; hardness 5–15 degrees; group of 6+ recommended; tight-fitting covers essential (jumpers); dark substrate and background advised; eggs hatch 14–20 days; fry require infusoria then brine shrimp; notes advanced care due to water quality sensitivity.

additional.name: Diszhal.info — Procatopus aberrans (Bluegreen Lampeye)
additional.notes: Cross-checked lifespan (1–2 years), temp 24–26°C, pH 6.5–7.2, hardness 4.5–11.2 NK°, minimum tank 120 L for 10 fish (60 L confirmed adequate for a small group), diet (live food, Artemia, flakes), native range (Cameroon, Nigeria), peaceful schooling behaviour.

additional.name: Killi.co.uk — Procatopus aberrans species profile
additional.notes: Killifish specialist source. Confirmed native range (Cameroon, Nigeria); neutral-water breeding success; fry accept micro worms and baby brine shrimp; diet includes white worms and fruit flies; adults will consume fry; slightly timid disposition; community tank only with fish of similar size.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer depositing 1–3 eggs daily in crevices/mops/cork bark. Incubation 14–40 days depending on temperature. Fry 4–5 mm; require infusoria/micro worms initially, then baby brine shrimp. Adults eat fry; spawning medium should be transferred to a separate rearing vessel. Stable soft acidic water is the key prerequisite.
